The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:tier 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

{{ill|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|ja|鹿島天足別命|12=draft|12=draft}} | established= Unknown | shrine rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its historical rank in the [[modern shrine ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its historical rank in the [[modern shrine ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kashima Miko Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Kashima-miko-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki city 1.JPG|250px|Haiden of Kashima Miko Shrine]] | location = 2-1-10 Hiyorigaoka, Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|38|25|26.3|N|141|18|26.3|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|ja|鹿島天足別命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | established= Unknown | shrine rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its historical rank in the [[modern shrine ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:Hondawake-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:March 28 orphaned pages]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kashima Miko Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Kashima-miko-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki city 1.JPG|250px|Haiden of Kashima Miko Shrine]] | location = 2-1-10 Hiyorigaoka, Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|38|25|26.3|N|141|18|26.3|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|ja|鹿島天足別命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | established= Unknown | shrine rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its historical rank in the [[modern shrine ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:retagging the orphans]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:ophans as of March 31]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its historical rank in the [[modern shrine ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill||Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kashima Miko Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Kashima-miko-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki city 1.JPG|250px|Haiden of Kashima Miko Shrine]] | location = 2-1-10 Hiyorigaoka, Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|38|25|26.3|N|141|18|26.3|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|ja|鹿島天足別命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} | established= Unknown | shrine Rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill||Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||12=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=draft}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}}

Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|12=draft|12=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ||qq=draft|qq=draft}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{citation needed|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kashima Miko Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|Mutsu Province|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|qq=}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Ishinomaki]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]]

御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|ja|石巻市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishinomaki|qq=}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province|2=ja|3=陸奥国|ja|Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province|2=ja|3=陸奥国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsu Province|qq=}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|武甕槌命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|qq=}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the [[2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]]. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ([[Emperor Ōjin|Hondawake-no-Mikoto]]). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|ja|カグツチ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shrines destroyed in the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami]] [[Category:Kashima shrines]] [[Category:orphaned pages as of April 8]] }} [[sn:Kashima Miko Shrine]] [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:ashima Miko Shrine]] [[en:Kashima Miko Shrine]]
