revision.text:
{{moved from|Kashima Miko Shrine}} [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kashima Miko Shrine | image = File:Haiden of Kashima-miko-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki city 1.JPG | location = 2-1-10 Hiyorigaoka, Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|38.423972|N|141.308111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} | established= Unknown | shrine Rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} | prefecture= {{ill|Hiyorigaoka|WD=Q109264829|1=Hiyorigaoka}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki|1=Ishinomaki}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]]

[[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kashima Miko Shrine | image = File:Haiden of Kashima-miko-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki city 1.JPG | location = 2-1-10 Hiyorigaoka, Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|38.423972|N|141.308111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690}} | established= Unknown | shrine Rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} | prefecture= {{ill|Hiyorigaoka|WD=Q109264829}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]

The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]] [[Category:Qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

[[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki]][[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kashima Miko Shrine | image = File:Haiden of Kashima-miko-jinja shrine,Ishinomaki city 1.JPG | location = 2-1-10 Hiyorigaoka, Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan | coordinates= {{Coord|38.423972|N|141.308111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|WD=Q2564508|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}; {{ill|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} | established= Unknown | shrine Rank= {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}} (minor shrine), Former Prefectural Shrine | main hall = Three-bay hip-and-gable structure ([[Irimoya-zukuri]]) | festivals = {{nihongo|festival|festival}} (May 15) | map = {{location map|Japan Miyagi|float=center|caption=Kashima Miko Shrine|lat_deg=38|lat_min=25|lat_sec=26.3|lon_deg=141|lon_min=18|lon_sec=26.3|label=鹿島
御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} | prefecture= {{ill|Hiyorigaoka|WD=Q109264829|1=Hiyorigaoka}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki|1=Ishinomaki}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]][[Category:Move targets ∪ destinations]] [[Category:Qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Qqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]]

御児神社|position=bottom|width=200}} | prefecture= {{ill|Hiyorigaoka|WD=Q109264829|1=Hiyorigaoka}} }} '''Kashima Miko Shrine''' ({{nihongo|鹿島御児神社|かしまみこじんじゃ}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Ishinomaki|en|Ishinomaki|WD=Q270814|lt=Ishinomaki|1=Ishinomaki}}, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan. It is listed among the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki_Jinmyocho}}, specifically as one of the ten shrines of {{nihongo|Oshika District|牡鹿郡}} in {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}}. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|WD=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. Its historical Rank in the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] was that of a prefectural shrine. == Deities == The shrine enshrines two deities: * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|qid=Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}}, the god of martial arts and protection. * {{nihongo|Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto|鹿島天足別命}}, the child of Takemikazuchi and a deity associated with regional development and maritime safety. == History == Although the exact founding date of Kashima Miko Shrine is unknown, it first appears in historical records in {{nihongo|859 AD|貞観元年}}. It is one of the shrines mentioned in the {{nihongo|Sandai Jitsuroku|三代実録}}, which lists 38 shrines dedicated to the progeny of Kashima Daimyojin across the Mutsu Province. The shrine is also referenced in the {{nihongo|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|延喜式神名帳}} as one of the ten shrines in Oshika District. According to legend, Takemikazuchi and his son Kashima Amatariwake no Mikoto played key roles in the pacification of the Tohoku region, under the guidance of {{ill|Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi-no-Oji|lt=Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja|塩土老翁神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiotsuchi no Oji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シオツチオジ}}, the god of navigation. The origin of Ishinomaki's name derives from a myth where Amatariwake's ship anchored in the region and pulled up stones, which were said to have "rolled stones" (石を巻く). The shrine gained prominence and became a center for regional development and the worship of martial deities, often receiving patronage from feudal lords. It was officially designated a village shrine in 1874, upgraded to a district shrine in 1921, and reached the Rank of a prefectural shrine in 1935. == Significance == Kashima Miko Shrine is regarded as a site of worship for protection, victory, safe childbirth, and maritime safety. Its association with Takemikazuchi makes it particularly popular among martial artists and those seeking strength and courage. == Architecture and Grounds == The shrine features a reconstructed three-bay hip-and-gable structure (Irimoya-zukuri) as its main hall, completed in 2019. A notable feature is the shrine's concrete torii gate, originally constructed in 1935 and damaged during the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|qid=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}. A new torii gate was built in 2021 using Akita cedar and decorated with Oga slate for enhanced durability. === Sub-shrines === * '''Hachiman Shrine''' – Dedicated to Emperor Ōjin ({{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|qid=Q317997|lt=Hondawake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}). * '''Tenmangu Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q382005}}. * '''Inari Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}, enshrined in 1935. * '''Atago Shrine''' – Dedicated to {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|en|Kagu-tsuchi|qid=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}, restored by local seafarers in the early Edo period. === Other Features === * Observation platform offering views of the Pacific Ocean and the Kitakami River. * A historical main hall donated by {{ill|Iseyama Kotaijingu|ja伊勢山皇大神宮|1=Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|2=fr|3=Iseyama Kotai Jingu|4=ja|5=伊勢山皇大神宮|6=zh|7=伊勢山皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kotaijingu|13=User:Immanuelle/Iseyama Kōtai Jingū|qq=}} in Yokohama. == Events == * '''festival (Reitaisai)''' – Held on May 15, featuring rituals for prosperity and safety. * '''Observances for Maritime Safety''' – Special ceremonies for sailors and fishermen. == Post-Earthquake Recovery == Following the devastation of the {{nihongo|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|東日本大震災}}, Kashima Miko Shrine underwent significant restoration. The shrine's historical artifacts and structures were preserved or reconstructed with community support, ensuring its continued role as a spiritual and cultural landmark in Ishinomaki. == References == {{Reflist}} == External Links == * [http://kashimamiko.org/ Official Website of Kashima Miko Shrine] {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|鹿島御児神社|version=98354991|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:鹿島御児神社]] {{Commons category|Kashima-miko-jinja, Ishinomaki}} {{wikidata link|Q17223553}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Ishinomaki, Miyagi]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mutsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]
